g.desc:
A technical line-art blueprint showing a completed Rover robot in a three-quarter perspective.
The Image: This is a reference graphic showing the final assembly of the Rover kit. The robot is composed of a long, flat wooden baseplate with a wide front and a narrow, tapered rear.
Key Components Shown:
Front: The Range Finder "eyes" are mounted vertically, secured to a wooden holder by a black O-ring.
Center: Two large, five-spoke wheels with ridged rubber tires are attached to motors beneath the base.
Top: The Motor Module circuit board is velcroed to the top surface, with motor wires disappearing into a central hole and a 4-pin cable extending toward the rear.
Rear: A ball caster assembly is mounted to the underside of the tail, providing a third point of contact with the ground.
Tactile & Mechanical Tips Visualizing the Goal: Use this image as a "North Star" for your assembly. Every step you complete should bring your physical hardware closer to this configuration.
Check the Stance: When finished, the Rover should sit level. If the front or back is dipping significantly, it usually indicates that the spacers for either the Motor Module or the Ball Caster were swapped or missed.
Symmetry: Notice how the wheels and motor spacers are perfectly mirrored on the left and right sides. If your robot feels lopsided, check the orientation of the wooden spacers from Step 3.
g.title: Example of a completed Rover

g.desc:
A technical line-art blueprint showing the assembly of the ultrasonic range finder sensor onto its wooden mounting holder.
The Action: The rectangular range finder sensor, with its two cylindrical "eyes," is positioned in front of the laser-cut wooden holder. A black O-ring is shown looped around the sensor, ready to be stretched over the holder's mounting notches.
The Connection: Two orange dashed arrows curve from the outer edges of the O-ring toward the two large, circular notches on the left and right sides of the wooden holder. This indicates that the rubber ring should be pulled back to hook onto the holder, securing the sensor against the flat wooden face.
Text Prompt: "Use O-Ring to attach Range Finder to Holder."
Tactile & Mechanical Tips Alignment: Before stretching the O-ring, ensure the two cylindrical components of the range finder are seated snugly into the semi-circular cutouts at the top of the wooden holder. The sensor board should sit flush against the wood.
O-Ring Tension: The O-ring provides the tension needed to hold the sensor in place without screws. Place the O-ring across the front of the sensor (between the two "eyes"), then stretch each side back simultaneously to hook them into the deep side notches of the holder.
Final Check: Once attached, the sensor should not wiggle. If it feels loose, check that the O-ring hasn't slipped out of the notches on the back of the wooden piece.
g.title: Rover Assembly Step 1A

g.desc:
A technical line-art blueprint showing the attachment of the Range Finder assembly to the main Rover baseplate.
The Action: The completed Range Finder and Holder sub-assembly is positioned vertically above the front edge of the Rover's flat wooden base.
The Connection: An orange dashed arrow points downward from the central wooden tab at the bottom of the holder toward a matching square slot in the baseplate. This indicates the tab should be inserted into the slot to mount the sensor assembly.
Text Prompt: "Insert Holder into Rover Base".
Tactile & Mechanical Tips Tab Alignment: The wooden holder has a single T-shaped tab at the bottom. Align this tab precisely with the square cutout on the front of the baseplate.
Seating the Part: Push the holder down firmly until the horizontal "shoulders" of the wooden piece sit flush against the top surface of the baseplate.
Stability Check: At this stage, the holder is held in place by friction. It should stand upright on its own, but it will not be fully secure until additional supports or hardware are added in later steps. Be careful not to knock it over while working on the rest of the chassis.
g.title: Rover Assembly Step 1B

g.desc:
A technical line-art blueprint showing the hardware installation to secure the Range Finder Holder to the Rover base.
The Action: Two #4-40 screws (labeled "A") are shown being inserted upward through the bottom of the baseplate. Two matching #4-40 hex nuts are positioned to meet the screws inside the T-shaped slots of the wooden holder.
The Connection: Orange dashed lines indicate the path of the screws through the circular holes in the base and into the vertical channels of the holder. A secondary "detail box" on the right shows a cross-section of the T-slot, illustrating how the nut sits horizontally in the wide part of the "T" while the screw threads into it from below.
Text Prompt: "Attach Holder using #4-40 Nuts".
Tactile & Mechanical Tips The T-Slot Technique: This assembly uses a "captive nut" joint. Slide the #4-40 hex nut into the horizontal cross-slot of the wooden holder first. You may need to hold it in place with a finger or a piece of tape while you insert the screw from the underside of the base.
Screw Identification: Ensure you are using the #4-40 Screws (A) identified in your inventory. These are thicker than the #2-56 screws and should fit snugly through the holes in the baseplate.
Finger Tight First: Turn the screws by hand until you feel them catch the threads of the nut. Once both are started, use a screwdriver to tighten them. The wooden parts should be pulled tight against each other with no visible gap.
Alignment Check: As you tighten, ensure the holder remains perpendicular (at a 90° angle) to the base.
g.title: Rover Assembly Step 1C

g.desc:
A technical line-art blueprint showing the preparation of the ball caster assembly for the Rover.
The Action: The "Ball and Holder" assembly is shown in an exploded view. Two #2-56 screws (labeled "B") are being inserted upward through the bottom of the plastic ball housing. Above the housing, two wooden spacers are stacked, waiting to be threaded onto the screws.
The Connection: Orange dashed vertical lines show the alignment path. The screws pass through the two small tabs on either side of the circular plastic housing, then through the matching holes in the two rectangular wooden spacers (#2 Spacers).
Text Prompt: "Remove Ball from Holder. Insert Screws as shown."
Tactile & Mechanical Tips Preparation: Before starting, pop the metal or plastic ball out of the plastic holder. This makes it easier to handle the housing and ensures the ball doesn't fall out and roll away while you are trying to align the screws.
Hardware Check: Use the #2-56 Screws (B). These are the thinner screws from your inventory. If you find the screw is too large for the holes in the plastic housing, double-check that you haven't accidentally grabbed the #4-40 screws.
Stacking Spacers: Hold the two wooden #2 Spacers together so their holes align perfectly. Place them on top of the plastic holder tabs. Slide the screws in from the bottom—the screws should pass through all three layers (one plastic, two wood) freely.
Orientation: The plastic holder has a specific "cup" shape. Ensure the open side of the cup is facing downward (away from the wooden spacers) so the ball can be re-inserted later to touch the floor.
g.title: Rover Assembly Step 2A

g.desc:
A technical line-art blueprint showing the final mounting of the ball caster assembly to the rear of the Rover baseplate.
The Action: The stack from the previous step (plastic holder, wooden spacers, and screws) is being lifted toward the underside of the tail end of the Rover base. Simultaneously, the caster ball is shown being pressed back into the plastic housing.
The Connection: Two orange dashed arrows point upward from the protruding #2-56 screw threads toward two small holes at the very back of the baseplate. A solid orange arrow points upward from the ball toward the circular opening of the plastic holder.
Text Prompt: "Attach stack to Base with Screws. Reinsert Ball into Holder."
Tactile & Mechanical Tips Rear Alignment: This assembly attaches to the narrow "tail" section of the baseplate. Align the protruding screws with the two small holes located behind the etched motor orientation markings (L and R).
Seating the Screws: The screws thread directly into the wooden baseplate. Use a screwdriver to turn the #2-56 screws from the bottom until the wooden spacers are cinched tight against the underside of the Rover base. The plastic housing should be the lowest point of the stack.
The "Click": When reinserting the ball, push it firmly into the plastic cup. You should feel it "snap" or click past the retaining tabs. Once inside, the ball should spin freely in all directions but should not fall out when the Rover is lifted.
Levelling: Once this step is complete, your Rover has its third point of contact with the ground. It should now be able to sit level on a flat surface, supported by the front edge and this rear ball caster.
g.title: Rover Assembly Step 2B

g.desc:
A technical line-art blueprint showing the installation of the motor spacers into the Rover base.
The Action: Two identical laser-cut wooden motor spacers are positioned below the wide, rectangular section of the Rover baseplate. They are being lifted vertically to join with the base.
The Connection: Three orange dashed arrows point upward, indicating how the notches on the spacers interlock with the notches on the base. The spacers are oriented perpendicular to the baseplate, creating a "slotted" joint.
Text Prompt: "Insert Motor Spacers into Base notches."
Tactile & Mechanical Tips Notch Alignment: The motor spacers and the baseplate are designed with matching rectangular cutouts. Align the vertical slots in the spacers with the horizontal slots on the sides of the baseplate.
Perpendicular Fit: Ensure the spacers are standing perfectly upright. They should slide into the baseplate until the top of the spacer notch hits the bottom of the baseplate notch.
Symmetry: There are two spacers, one for the left and one for the right. Make sure they are both fully seated; the wooden tabs should protrude slightly through the top of the baseplate.
Temporary Friction: These parts may feel a bit loose until the motor holder and motors are added in the next steps to "lock" the structure together. For now, just ensure they are pushed in as far as they can go.
g.title: Rover Assembly Step 3

g.desc:
A technical line-art blueprint showing the installation of the geared motors and the motor holder onto the Rover chassis.
The Action: Two FM90 geared motors are being lowered into the channels created by the motor spacers on the baseplate. Above them, a rectangular wooden motor holder and two long #4-40 screws (labeled "C") are positioned to secure the assembly.
The Connection: Multiple orange dashed lines show the vertical alignment. The motors seat into the notches of the spacers. The red and black wires from both motors are shown being threaded through a large circular hole in the center of the baseplate. The two long screws pass through the motor holder, through the mounting tabs on the motors, and into the baseplate.
Text Prompt: "Orient Motors so wires extend through the hole as shown."
Tactile & Mechanical Tips Wire Management: This is the most critical part of this step. Before seating the motors, gather the wires and push them through the large center hole from the bottom to the top. This keeps the wires safe and prevents them from being pinched between the motor and the wooden frame.
Motor Orientation: Ensure the white output shafts (where the wheels will attach) are facing outward toward the left and right sides of the Rover. The "back" of the motors (where the wires come out) should face the center hole.
Hardware Identification: Use the #4-40 Screws (C). These are the longest screws in the kit, designed to pass through the holder and the entire body of the motor.
The "Sandwich" Fit: The motor holder acts as a cap. Once the screws are tightened, the motors should be "sandwiched" firmly between the top holder and the bottom baseplate. If the motors can still wiggle, check that the screws are fully tightened into the base.
g.title: Rover Assembly Step 4

g.desc:
A technical line-art blueprint showing the assembly of the wheels and tires onto the Rover's motor shafts.
The Action: A rubber tire (labeled "Wheel") is being stretched over the circumference of a plastic "Rim." Once the tire is seated, the entire wheel assembly is aligned with the white output shaft of the motor. A small screw (labeled "D") is positioned to be driven through the center of the rim into the motor shaft.
The Connection: Small orange arrows indicate the tire stretching over the rim. A larger orange dashed arrow shows the center hole of the rim sliding onto the ridged motor shaft. Finally, an arrow shows the small screw (D) entering the center of the wheel assembly to lock it in place.
Text Prompt: "Stretch the Wheels over the Rims. Insert the Motor shaft into the ridged hole of the Wheel."
Tactile & Mechanical Tips Tire Installation: The tires are flexible rubber. Use your thumbs to stretch the tire over one edge of the rim first, then work your way around the circle until the inner lip of the tire "snaps" into the groove on the rim. It should fit tightly with no bulging.
Shaft Alignment: The center hole of the plastic rim is not perfectly circular; it has flat sides or "ridges" to match the motor shaft. Rotate the wheel slowly while pushing it onto the shaft until you feel the ridges interlock and the wheel slides all the way on.
Hardware Identification: Use the Small Screws (D). These are the shortest screws in your kit. Their purpose is to prevent the wheel from sliding off the shaft during movement.
Avoid Over-tightening: Tighten screw (D) until it is snug against the plastic rim. Do not over-tighten, as you could crack the plastic center of the wheel or strip the internal threads of the motor shaft.
g.title: Rover Assembly Step 5

g.desc:
A technical line-art blueprint showing the electrical connection between the geared motors and the Motor Module.
The Action: The red and black motor wires, which were previously threaded through the center hole of the baseplate, are shown being plugged into two small ports on the Motor Module circuit board. A 4-pin cable is already connected to the white port on the opposite end of the module.
The Connection: Two orange arrows point from the small plastic connectors at the end of the motor wires toward two matching metal headers on the surface of the green PCB. This indicates the wires should be plugged in firmly to establish power.
Text Prompt: "Connect wires to Motor Module."
Tactile & Mechanical Tips Connector Orientation: The small plastic connectors on the ends of the motor wires are polarized, meaning they only fit one way. Look for the small metal "teeth" on the connector and align them with the opening in the module's port. If it doesn't slide in easily, do not force it; flip it over and try again.
Firm Seating: Push the connectors until you feel a slight "click" or until they are fully seated against the base of the port. A loose connection here can cause one motor to stall or stutter while the Rover is moving.
Cable Strain: Since these wires are coming up through the baseplate, ensure there is enough slack so that the Motor Module can sit comfortably on the chassis without pulling on the wires.
Port Selection: It typically does not matter which motor goes into which of the two small ports on this specific module, as the software will define the "Left" and "Right" directions later.
g.title: Rover Assembly Step 6A

g.desc:
A technical line-art blueprint showing the mounting of the Motor Module to the top of the Rover chassis.
The Action: The Motor Module circuit board is shown being lowered toward the wooden baseplate. Two square Velcro pieces are positioned between the board and the base, ready to create a removable adhesive bond.
The Connection: Orange dashed vertical arrows show the alignment of the "sandwich" assembly. One Velcro square attaches to the underside of the Motor Module PCB, and the matching Velcro square attaches to the flat surface of the baseplate, just behind the wire entry hole.
Text Prompt: "Attach Motor Module to Base with Velcro Piece".
Tactile & Mechanical Tips Surface Preparation: Before sticking the Velcro down, ensure the wooden baseplate and the underside of the Motor Module are free of dust or wood shavings from the laser-cutting process. This will ensure the adhesive holds strongly.
Peel and Stick: Peel the protective backing from one Velcro square and press it firmly to the center of the underside of the PCB. Peel the backing from the second square and press it onto the wooden baseplate.
The "Hook and Loop" Bond: Press the Motor Module down onto the baseplate Velcro. You should feel the two sides interlock. Using Velcro instead of screws allows you to easily remove the module later if you need to adjust the wire management or troubleshoot the connections.
g.title: Rover Assembly Step 6B

g.desc:
A technical line-art blueprint showing the fully assembled Rover robot chassis.
The Action: The completed Rover is shown in a three-quarter perspective. It features the range finder "eyes" mounted at the front, the large wheels and motors in the center, and the ball caster supporting the rear. The Motor Module is securely velcroed to the top, with the 4-pin cable extending toward the back of the baseplate.
The Connection: All components from previous steps—the laser-cut wood frame, the brass hardware, the electronic sensors, and the rubber tires—are now integrated into a single, cohesive unit.
Text Prompt: "ASSEMBLY COMPLETE! Head to Piper Make to continue your mission."
Tactile & Mechanical Tips Final Mobility Check: Place the Rover on a flat surface and give it a gentle push. It should roll smoothly on its two main wheels and the rear ball caster. If it wobbles or drags, check that the rear caster ball is seated correctly and that the motor screws (C) are tight.
Tire Clearance: Ensure the rubber tires are not rubbing against the wooden motor spacers or the baseplate. If they are, slide the wheel slightly outward on the motor shaft before re-tightening screw (D).
Wire Safety: Confirm that the red and black motor wires are tucked neatly through the center hole and aren't dangling where they could get caught in the rotating wheels or the gears.
Ready for Power: With the chassis complete, the 4-pin cable is now ready to be connected to your Breadboard and Raspberry Pi Pico to begin programming your mission.
g.title: Assembly Complete
